plainMeaning: Homage to the awakened ones whose supreme knowledge and vision can never be blocked, and who have shed the veil that once hid their full awareness.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit appaḍihaya < Sanskrit apratihata ('not struck back, unobstructed'): a- (not) + prati-hata (struck back, repelled).
wordByWord.notes: Technical sense: knowledge and vision unobstructed in substance, place, time, and mode (dravya-kṣetra-kāla-bhāva) — i.e. kevala (absolute).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit/Sanskrit vara, from √vṛ 'to choose, to prefer'; hence 'the choicest, the best'.
Prakrit nāṇa < Sanskrit jñāna, from √jñā 'to know'.
wordByWord.notes: In the compound vara-nāṇa = 'supreme knowledge', signifying omniscience.

wordByWord.gloss: vision, perception, intuition (here: kevala-darśana, absolute perception)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit daṁsaṇa < Sanskrit darśana, from √dṛś 'to see'.
wordByWord.notes: Jñāna and darśana form the doctrinal pair: indeterminate apprehension (darśana) and determinate cognition (jñāna).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit dhara < Sanskrit dhara, from √dhṛ 'to hold, bear, sustain'; gen./dat. pl. honorific ending -āṇaṁ (Skt. -ānām).
wordByWord.notes: Continues the homage-dative governed by 'namotthuṇaṁ' (v.2): 'to the holders of…'. The whole compound: appaḍihaya-vara-nāṇa-daṁsaṇa-dharāṇaṁ = 'to those who bear unobstructed supreme knowledge-and-vision'.

wordByWord.gloss: turned away from, cast off, gone past
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit viaṭṭa < Sanskrit vyāvṛtta, from vi-ā-√vṛt 'to turn away, revolve away, withdraw'.
wordByWord.notes: Some glossators read vyāvartita ('removed, dispelled'); either way the sense is that the chadmastha condition has been left behind.

wordByWord.gloss: the veil — the chadmastha (still-veiled, non-omniscient) state
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit chaüma / chaümma < Sanskrit chadman ('cover, veil, disguise'), from √chad 'to cover'; cf. chadmastha 'situated in the veil'.
wordByWord.notes: chadma = the cover of the four destructive (ghātī) karmas, especially the knowledge- and perception-obscuring karmas; see glossary 'chadmastha'.

wordByWord.gloss: to those (who…) — honorific genitive/dative plural ending
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit -āṇaṁ < Sanskrit -ānām (gen. pl.), used here in the dative-of-homage sense governed by namotthuṇaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Carried by both dharāṇaṁ and chaümāṇaṁ, binding this verse into the running litany of datives. viaṭṭa-chaümāṇaṁ = 'to those from whom the veil has been turned away'.

literalTranslation: To the bearers of unobstructed, supreme knowledge-and-vision; to those who have turned away from (cast off) the veiling (chadmastha) state.
idiomaticTranslation: Reverence to them — holders of supreme knowledge and perception that nothing can obstruct, who have forever left behind the veiled, partial state of the unenlightened.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This single line is built on a deliberate two-beat logic: first the positive possession, then the negation of its opposite. 'Appaḍihaya-vara-nāṇa-daṁsaṇa-dharāṇaṁ' affirms what the Arihantas hold — kevala-jñāna and kevala-darśana, 'supreme' (vara) and 'unobstructed' (appaḍihaya) because they meet no barrier of object, distance, time, or mode. 'Viaṭṭa-chaümāṇaṁ' then states the same fact from the reverse side: the chadma — the karmic veil that keeps ordinary souls 'chadmastha' — has been turned away (viaṭṭa). The two clauses are not separate praises but a single truth seen from both faces: knowledge shines without limit precisely because the cover is gone. Note the economy: where the previous verse heaped the five 'dhamma-' sovereign titles, this verse drops all ornament and names the inner achievement that those public titles rest upon — total cognition. It thereby prepares the great reflexive couplets that follow (jiṇa-jāvaya, tinna-tāraya…), for only one who has himself crossed the veil can carry others across. The pairing of jñāna with darśana is exact Jain usage: darśana is the soul's undifferentiated 'seeing', jñāna its determinate 'knowing'; in the kevalin both are complete and operate over the whole of reality.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse pinpoints the threshold between chadmastha and kevalin. A soul is chadmastha so long as the four ghātī (destructive) karmas — and above all the jñānāvaraṇīya (knowledge-obscuring) and darśanāvaraṇīya (perception-obscuring) karmas — still 'cover' it. When they are annihilated, kevala-jñāna and kevala-darśana arise together and 'unobstructedly' (apratihata): this is the move from the lower guṇasthānas to the thirteenth (sayogi-kevalī). 'Appaḍihaya' is therefore a precise term, not a flourish — Jain epistemology holds that the soul's knowing is innately infinite and is only ever veiled, never created; remove the veil (viaṭṭa-chaüma) and the infinite stands revealed. The verse thus encodes the doctrine that omniscience is unveiling, not acquisition.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For the reciter, this line reframes one's own ignorance: knowledge is not lacking but covered. Bowing to those who 'cast off the veil' is bowing to the destiny latent in oneself, and a reminder that spiritual practice is the thinning of karmic cover, not the importing of something foreign.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 10.1 — 'mohakṣayāj jñāna-darśanāvaraṇāntarāya-kṣayāc ca kevalam' (omniscience arises on the destruction of the deluding, knowledge-, perception-obscuring and obstructive karmas)
Namutthuṇaṁ as embedded in the Aupapātika (Uvavāiya) sūtra — canonical home of this clause-litany

sources.title: Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Hindi Dictionary), H. D. T. Sheth
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit glosses: appaḍihaya (= apratihata), viaṭṭa (= vyāvṛtta), chaüma (= chadman), and the gen./dat. honorific ending -āṇaṁ.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots underlying the tokens: apratihata (a-prati-√han), vyāvṛtta (vi-ā-√vṛt), chadman (√chad), jñāna (√jñā), darśana (√dṛś).

sources.title: Namaskāra / Śakra-stava commentary and Āgama attestation (jainqq.org digital library)
sources.consultedFor: Confirmation of the clause as a praise of kevala-jñāna/kevala-darśana and the chadmastha→kevalin distinction in the Namutthuṇaṁ.

notes: The verse is clause 7 of the Namutthuṇaṁ (printed v.8/number 7). Reading of 'viaṭṭa': taken as Skt. vyāvṛtta ('turned away from'); a minority of glossators give vyāvartita ('removed/dispelled') — the doctrinal sense (the chadma-veil is gone) is identical. 'chaüma' is glossed via the preface term chadmastha and not re-defined here. The printed source word-spacing (વરનાણ–દંસણધરાણં as a dashed compound) is preserved; tokens are split by morpheme for full coverage. Note: a non-Jain web source mis-glossed the line as Pali / as 'four kinds of pride (catur-māna)' — that reading is rejected; in this Śvetāmbara liturgical context -māṇaṁ is the case-ending of chaüma, not māna 'pride'.
